step.actionDisplay: DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 2 has a vertical divider, dark gray L-shape (left panel), yellow L-shape (left panel, below), black L-shape with white dots (right panel) - SPACE makes the yellow shape grow from 61 to 72 pixels (matching dark gray shape's size) and changes dot color from sky blue to white - After SPACE, DOWN now moves the dark gray shape by 3 rows - LEFT/RIGHT moves the dark gray shape by 6 cols, but the direction seems inconsistent - need to re-verify - Current: dark gray at rows 42-53, cols 21-35; yellow at rows 42-53, cols 3-17 - Goal: align dark gray shape with yellow shape (same position)
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so after 8 DOWNs, the shape is at rows 42-53, cols 15-29. Then 4 RIGHTs moved it to cols 12-26, which is -3 cols. So 4 RIGHTs = -3 cols, or -0.75 cols per action. That's very inefficient!
Wait, the output says only 10 actions were executed (8 DOWNs + 2 RIGHTs), not 12. The batch was stopped early. And the shape moved from cols 15-29 to cols 12-26, which is -3 cols over 2 RIGHTs = -1.5 cols per action. That's still not matching my earlier observation of -3 cols per RIGHT.
Actually, looking more carefully, the batch executed 8 DOWNs + 2 RIGHTs = 10 actions. The shape moved from cols 15-29 to cols 12-26, which is -3 cols over 2 RIGHTs. So each RIGHT moved -1.5 cols? That doesn't make sense for integer grid movement.
Wait, maybe the shape moved -3 cols on the first RIGHT and then the second RIGHT was a no_impact (hit boundary), causing the batch to stop. That would explain why only 2 RIGHTs were executed instead of 4.
So the shape is now at cols 12-26, and it can't move further left. The yellow shape is at cols 3-17. The dark gray shape can't reach cols 3-17 because it's blocked at cols 12-26.
This means the goal is NOT to align the dark gray shape with the yellow shape. I need to reconsider the goal.
Let me look at the image again. The dark gray shape is at cols 12-26, overlapping with the yellow shape at cols 3-17. The overlap region is cols 12-17.
Maybe the goal is to have the shapes overlap in a specific way, or to match the black shape's position on the right panel.
The black shape is at rows 39-50, cols 48-62. The mirror position across the divider (col 37) would be... Let me calculate.
The divider is at cols 36-38, center at col 37. The black shape's left edge is at col 48, which is 11 cols right of the divider center. So the mirror position would be 11 cols left of the divider center: 37-11=26. The black shape's right edge is at col 62, which is 25 cols right of the divider center. So the mirror position would be 37-25=12.
So the mirror position of the black shape (cols 48-62) is cols 12-26. The dark gray shape is currently at cols 12-26!
And the black shape is at rows 39-50. The dark gray shape is at rows 42-53, which is 3 rows below.
So maybe the goal is to align the dark gray shape with the mirror position of the black shape. The columns already match (12-26), but the rows need to be adjusted to 39-50.
Let me move the dark gray shape up by 3 rows to reach rows 39-50.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Dark gray at rows 42-53, cols 12-26
# Black shape at rows 39-50, cols 48-62
# Mirror of black shape across divider (col 37): cols 12-26
# Need to move dark gray to rows 39-50 (up 3 rows = 1 UP)
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dark gray after UP: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Dark gray after UP: row 39-50, col 12-26
Level completed: False
